name: Empress Jingu
description: Empress Jingu is a legendary figure in Japanese history, traditionally believed to have ruled from 201 to 269 AD. Accounts of her life are shrouded in myth and legend, making it difficult to separate historical fact from embellished narrative. She is best known for her alleged conquest of Korea, a feat that solidified her image as a powerful and ambitious ruler. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the two oldest chronicles of Japan, Jingu was the wife of Emperor Chuai. When her husband questioned the gods' prediction of a Korean invasion, he died, leading Jingu to take control. She is said to have led a successful military campaign to conquer Silla (one of the Korean kingdoms) while pregnant with her son, Emperor Ojin. This legendary campaign is often interpreted as a symbol of Japanese imperial ambitions and the divine mandate to rule. While the historicity of Empress Jingu and her Korean conquest is heavily debated by historians, her figure remains a significant part of Japanese cultural identity and national mythology. She embodies female leadership, military prowess, and divine favor, inspiring generations with her legendary exploits. Her story continues to be retold in literature, art, and popular culture, solidifying her place as a prominent and enduring figure in Japanese history and folklore.
subtitle: Legendary Empress and Conqueror of Korea

keyMyths.title: The Korean Conquest
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingu, while pregnant, led a successful campaign to conquer the Korean kingdom of Silla. This victory is attributed to her divine favor and strategic brilliance, securing Japanese dominance in the region.

keyMyths.title: Death of Emperor Chuai
keyMyths.description: When Emperor Chuai doubted the gods' prophecy of Korean conquest, he died. Empress Jingu took over the reins of power, interpreting this event as a divine sign of her destiny.

keyMyths.title: Birth of Emperor Ojin
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingu miraculously delayed her pregnancy for three years to ensure the success of her Korean campaign. After her return, she gave birth to Emperor Ojin, who would later become deified as Hachiman.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The oldest chronicle of Japan, containing myths, legends, and historical accounts of the imperial lineage.
